tax: Placed at genus rank, Chalk-Kin is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a calcium-carbonate body maintained by seep chemistry, not bulk earth-pressure alone. A new species belongs here only if it shows a calcite body with its own seep source and growth habit.
ap: The grouping is recognised by a hollow tufa tower or a shimmering flat crust on damp stone. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — a white lime film and either a hollow ring when struck or a clicking advance — which survives even when the body disperses.
eco: Chalk-Kin occupies tufa terraces and lime-drip caverns. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where a calcium-supersaturated seep deposits a calcite nucleus and recur with the seep or drip continues feeding the nucleus, sustaining themselves on lime supersaturation in seep or drip.
beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: poses as natural travertine or flowstone, jets or spreads on approach. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
traitsText: Shared characters: a calcium-carbonate body, acid-immunity in dilute doses, and strong-acid failure. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each species and species beneath this node.
abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: jetting lime-water or spreading sealing crust. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
bg: In the Throat, Chalk-Kin matters because makes lime-seep terraces and drip caverns caustic or sealing hazards. It lets survey reports sort calcareous hazards by habit, so a crew knows whether to wear a visor or test the floor without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.
g: Chalk-Kin is the genus-rank grouping of calcareous precipitate elementals of the calcite and tufa kind within the True Elementals. Its members share a calcium-carbonate body fed by lime-supersaturated seep or drip, and divide below into species groups by whether the calcite grows hollow and vertical or flat and spreading. The chalk kin bridge the mineral and the aqueous: their bodies are stone deposited by water.
